editableNewDialogue.text: Hi! Quick question—what’s the difference between USB-C and Thunderbolt?

USB-C is mainly a connector shape (the oval port). What it can do depends on the laptop/port: it might support only USB data, or also video (DisplayPort Alt Mode) and charging (USB Power Delivery). Thunderbolt (usually TB3/TB4) uses the USB-C connector but guarantees higher bandwidth and features like daisy-chaining and more reliable multi-monitor docking. A simple check is to look for a lightning-bolt icon near the port or in the specs.
If I buy a dock that says “USB-C”, will it always work with two monitors?

Not always. Two-monitor support depends on (1) whether the USB-C port supports video output, (2) whether the dock uses DisplayPort Alt Mode vs DisplayLink (USB graphics), and (3) the laptop’s GPU/OS limits. Thunderbolt docks more often handle dual monitors smoothly. If you paste the laptop model + dock model (or their spec pages), I can tell you what to expect.
Random: I heard the EU is standardizing charging ports—does that affect laptops too?

The EU rule is best known for pushing phones and many small electronics toward USB-C charging. For laptops, USB-C Power Delivery has already become common, but the regulation doesn’t force every laptop to charge via USB-C in all cases. In practice, you’ll still see many laptops that can charge over USB-C, while some models keep a proprietary barrel charger alongside it.
Got it. Also: does monitor “Ambilight” exist outside Philips?

Built-in “Ambilight” is a Philips branding feature and is mostly found on Philips displays/TVs. Outside that, you can replicate the effect with external bias-lighting kits (LED strips + controller) that react to screen content, but it won’t be branded Ambilight.

Btw, I’m rearranging my desk and realized the wall behind the monitor is just a window, so any backlight/ambilight effect would be kinda wasted—maybe I should stop obsessing over it and focus on ergonomics instead.

That makes sense—bias lighting/“ambilight” works best when it can reflect off a neutral wall. If you’ve got a window right behind the monitor, prioritizing ergonomics (stand adjustability), text clarity (PPI), and eye-comfort features (flicker-free + low blue light) will usually pay off more.
Actually, small correction: I’m not limiting myself to Polish stores anymore. I can buy from EU shops too if the price and warranty are good.
Got it — so it’s not “only Polish stores” now. We can include EU retailers as well, and just compare things like warranty terms, keyboard layout, and shipping/returns.
Yes, exactly. Polish stores are still nice, but not a hard requirement.
